petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented tile and stone business seeking to classify the beneficiary as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. The beneficiary works as a fabricator/installer for the petitioner's tile and stone business. The petitioner initially submitted only a statement attesting to the beneficiary's job duties and skills, and on appeal asserted that the beneficiary has reached a level whereby he can work unsupervised.
outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to submit documentation relating to any of the ten regulatory criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). The petitioner submitted only a statement about the beneficiary's job duties and skills initially, and on appeal submitted only a statement and a blank pay stub while offering to submit photographs. Even assuming the offered photographs might demonstrate that the beneficiary is a talented tile and stone fabricator/installer, the record does not reflect that he has attained any national acclaim for that talent or distinguished himself to such an extent that he may be said to be within the small percentage at the very top of his field. The burden of proof remains with the petitioner, and the petitioner has not sustained that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The petitioner was self-represented and submitted minimal evidence: initially only a statement of job duties and skills, and on appeal only a statement and a blank pay stub, while merely offering to submit photographs rather than actually submitting them. The petitioner failed to submit any documentation relating to the ten regulatory criteria.
